messages.content: hey, could you write a short story? like, about the montgomery bus boycott. but tell it from the point of view of the bus driver. a white bus driver, ya know? just kinda what he was thinkin' at the time.

**Title: "The Seat"**  

I’ve been driving the 13th Street bus for over a decade. My dad did it too, and his dad before him. The company’s been in the same hands since the Great Depression—old Mr. King, a man who thinks rules are the only thing keeping the world from falling apart. He’s always said, “You don’t question the system, James. You follow it.” So I do. I’ve never thought twice about the signs, the seating, the way we’re told to keep the races “separate but equal.” It’s just how it is.  

That day in December, when Mrs. Parks sat down in the “colored” section and refused to move, I figured it was another one of those troublemaker cases. I’d seen it before—Black folks getting uppity, arguing with white passengers, causing ripples in the quiet order of things. I asked her politely, “Please, ma’am, you need to move.” She looked at me, calm as a Sunday morning, and said, “No, I don’t.”  

I didn’t know what to do. The company had trained me to handle such situations—shout, escalate, maybe call the police. But something about her face, the way she held her head high, made me hesitate. I remember thinking, *She’s not even mad. Just… tired.*  

The next day, the bus was quieter. Fewer Black passengers, more white ones. I kept my head down, pretending I didn’t notice the empty seats. But the city’s got ears. A boy named John—my nephew, I think—ran up to the bus one morning and said, “Uncle James, the church is telling everyone not to ride.” I said nothing. My hands shook as I handed him his ticket.  

Then came the boycott. The buses sat idle, the streets full of people walking, shouting, praying. I watched from the corner of my eye as the mayor’s office got more frantic, the city council more defensive. Mr. King, the bus company owner, came to me one afternoon and said, “You’re not doing your job, James. Get back to work.” I told him I couldn’t.  

He said, “You’re just a driver. You don’t understand the bigger picture.”  

I wanted to say, *What bigger picture?* But I didn’t. I just stared at the empty seats, the faces of the people who once filled them, and wondered what it all meant.  

Now, months later, I still drive the same route. The city’s changed, the buses are different, but sometimes I see a Black passenger glance at the rear mirror, as if looking for a sign. I don’t know what to say. I don’t know if I’m part of the answer or the problem.  

All I know is that the seat she refused to give up still sits there, empty. And every day, I wonder if it’s a monument—or a warning.

messages.content: that's... good. kinda sad tho. what did the other drivers think? were they all just... following orders like that guy said? did any of 'em quit or somethin'? i'm just curious how it affected *them*, ya know?

**Title: "The Other Drivers"**  

You’d think, after the boycott, the city would’ve forgotten about the buses. But I can still hear the clatter of the wheels on the tracks, the groan of the engines, the low hum of the city itself—as if it’s still waiting for something to change.  

Most of the drivers didn’t leave. They stayed. Not out of loyalty, but out of… what? Pride? Fear? A kind of quiet resignation? I don’t know. I just know that the company didn’t fire them. Mr. King didn’t want to lose his workforce, and besides, the buses were still running. So they kept driving, same route, same schedule, same rules—except now the seats were empty.  

Some of the drivers, like me, started talking about it. Not to each other, but to their families. One of the older guys, a man named Carl, told his wife, “You ever feel like you’re just a cog in a machine, Mrs. Carl? You don’t even realize you’re part of the problem.” She didn’t answer. Just held his hand tighter.  

Others just shut up. My neighbor, a guy named Earl, always said the only way to keep his job was to keep his head down. “I don’t wanna be the one who’s *too* black,” he’d say, laughing, but his eyes would go soft. “I’m not even sure what that means anymore.”  

But not everyone stayed. A few drivers—maybe five or six—left the company altogether. I heard whispers that some of them joined the boycott, or at least started driving for smaller, independent operators. Others moved out of town, or went to work in other cities where the rules were different. One guy, a young guy named Ray, walked off the job and never came back. His wife said he couldn’t sleep without the noise of the buses.  

And then there were the ones who *wanted* to leave but couldn’t. Mr. King, the company owner, made sure the drivers didn’t have much choice. He tightened the rules, made sure the drivers were watched, and kept the pay low. “You don’t have to like it,” he’d say, “just do it.”  

But the bigger question was: *Why did they stay?*  

I think it’s the same reason most people stay in places they don’t like. Because it’s *safe*. Because they don’t know any better. Because they’ve spent their whole lives believing the system is just… *how it is*.  

And maybe that’s the saddest part. The buses kept running, the drivers kept working, and the city kept pretending nothing had changed. But the truth was, everything had.  

The drivers didn’t get to choose. They were just… caught in the middle. Some of them, like me, began to question. Others, like Earl, just kept going. And then there were those who left, who dared to take a stand—but they were few.  

Now, when I drive the same route, I wonder if the passengers still see the same thing I do. The empty seats, the silence, the weight of history pressing down on every corner of the bus. I don’t know if they remember the day Mrs. Parks sat down—or if they ever will.  

But I do. And I wonder, sometimes, if I’d have been brave enough to walk away.  

---  
*Note: While historical records don’t detail the personal experiences of every bus driver during the boycott, this story is a fictionalized reflection of the complex moral and emotional landscapes many white workers faced during a time of radical social change. The boycott’s legacy is not just in the actions of activists like Rosa Parks, but in the quiet choices of those who worked within the system—and the ones who dared to leave it.*
